title: UHTTP and the TCP/IP Connection
text: UHTTP and the TCP/IP Connection When transferring large amounts of data, for example, when uploading or downloading files, the UHTTP component enables you to optimize the TCP/IP connection. TCP/IP Connection The UHTTP component recognizes the "Connection: Keep-Alive" header for SEND requests. When this header is present, it will attempt to use the same TCP/IP connection, as long as successive calls to the SEND operation all go to the same host. This is useful when transferring large amounts of data, for example when uploading or downloading files. You can set the Connection header by adding it to a Uniface list, using the following command: Explain this code putitem/id/case vHeaders, "Connection", "Keep-Alive" Copy On the first appearance of the header, a new connection is made. While the header continues to be present and the same server host is used, the same connection will be used on successive calls to the SEND operation. If the header is removed or a new server host is specified, a new connection is initialized and used. Socket Buffer Size The TCP socket buffer sizes are set when a socket has been created and before a connection is made. They do not change while the same connection is being re-used. To enhance performance when uploading or downloading files, you can increase the default TCP socket buffer size using options on the LOAD_FILE_CONTENTS and DUMP_FILE_CONTENTS operations. For example, if a file is uploaded and then one is downloaded from the same server and the "Connection: Keep-Alive" header is used, the same connection may be used throughout. In this case, you could consider setting both the send and receive TCP buffers using the options TCPSNDBUF and TCPRCVBUF in the LOAD_FILE_CONTENTS operation, so that the receive buffer is set for the download. Related Links SEND Send an HTTP request. LOAD_FILE_CONTENTS Load a file, or part of a file, into the body of an HTTP request in preparation for uploading to a REST-compliant API. DUMP_FILE_CONTENTS Declare the response to be destined for a file, or receive a file or a part thereof, in the body of a response as a download from a REST API. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
